responses.strategic_rationale: This early-stage residential research facility navigates three core tensions: (1) ecological validity versus operational complexity—a domestic environment captures naturalistic parasomnia behavior but demands continuous safety monitoring and real-time technician annotation; (2) sensor comprehensiveness versus participant burden—tiered acquisition (low-burden nightly + scheduled enhanced PSG) balances physiological resolution with participant retention and data quality; (3) scientific ambition versus funding scarcity—the 3-year trajectory prioritizes methods validation and phenotyping (Aims 1–2) while deferring intervention studies, yet must prove sufficient event capture and staffing sustainability within tight enrollment and budget constraints. The selected levers address the most critical blockers: participant recruitment yield, technician capacity under variable event rates, facility utilization economics, data annotation throughput, and semi-automated triage development. These levers directly determine whether the unit achieves its 24-month viability threshold and generates publications sufficient for phase-two funding.

levers.name: Recruitment Pathway Intensity and Clinical Gatekeeping
levers.consequences: Immediate: Stricter pre-admission diagnostic confirmation (polysomnography + collateral history + PI adjudication) reduces enrollment velocity but eliminates marginal cases and nocturnal seizure mimics that would consume technician time on non-parasomnia events. Systemic: A 15–20% reduction in false-inclusion diagnoses lowers unproductive admission days by an estimated 8–12 days per cohort of 30 participants, freeing ~€2.4K in participant compensation and technician overtime for higher-yield recruitment channels. Strategic: Early diagnostic rigor builds methodological credibility for Aim 2 phenotyping publications but risks missing the month-18 enrollment target (25+ participants) if referral networks perceive barriers too high, forcing protocol relaxation midway.
options: Require documented polysomnography from referring clinician or conduct screening PSG for all pre-admission candidates, with PI final adjudication on ICSD-3 diagnostic criteria and seizure-mimicry exclusion, accepting 4–6 week intake delays.

Partner with University Hospital Bonn's sleep clinic for fast-track referral of clinically confirmed NREM parasomnia patients already in treatment, supplemented by regional neurology networks, balancing speed with selectivity.

Implement rapid telephone-based diagnostic interview by the clinical psychologist during initial contact, fast-tracking low-risk candidates to admission while reserving formal PSG for ambiguous cases, accelerating enrollment but increasing post-hoc exclusion risk.
levers.review_lever: Controls diagnostic certainty vs. recruitment velocity. Weakness: The options fail to consider how pre-admission dropout (participants who decline after detailed screening) scales with pathway stringency, potentially offsetting savings in unproductive admissions.

levers.name: Night Technician Staffing and Event-Response Scalability
levers.consequences: Immediate: Current 3-technician roster covers 8 active suites with 1.33 technicians per night on rotating shifts, allowing real-time annotation and event escalation; each technician performs 1–2 enhanced PSG hookups and monitors ~2.7 suites simultaneously during sleep. Systemic: If event frequency per participant averages 3 episodes per 2-week stay (consistent with inclusion criterion of ≥2/month), and escalation nights demand 45 min per flagged event, technician overtime scales nonlinearly; at 70% event-capture rate across 35 participants in year 2, estimated overtime burden rises to 380 hours (€5.7K additional cost) if staffing remains static, or forces deprioritization of real-time scoring. Strategic: Adding a 4th rotating technician (+€35K year 2) sustains safe coverage margins and timely annotation through 12-suite expansion, but delays cost-neutral single-shift operations; deferring 4th hire risks missed events and degraded data quality if event clustering coincides with vacation or illness.
options: Maintain 3-technician roster through year 2 with external freelance polysomnography technicians contracted on-demand for escalation nights, capping fixed overhead while accepting scheduling unpredictability and onboarding variability.

Hire a 4th technician on a fixed overnight contract starting Q2 year 2, aligned with projected enrollment ramp, distributing suite load to <2 per technician and building operational redundancy before 12-suite capacity expansion.

Implement a hybrid model where lead technicians train and supervise 2–3 rotating per-diem staff rotating on 2–3 night blocks per week, creating a tiered wage structure (reduces per-person cost) but introducing training overhead and quality inconsistency.
levers.review_lever: Controls labor cost vs. operational safety margin. Weakness: The options fail to consider how technician fatigue and annotation accuracy degrade under sustained overtime, potentially biasing event-triage algorithm training on low-quality scored data.

levers.name: Tiered PSG Scheduling and Targeted Escalation Protocol
levers.consequences: Immediate: Current plan schedules full PSG montage on admission night, mid-stay (day 10–14), and discharge; technician-flagged events trigger unscheduled escalation nights within 48 h. Systemic: Over a 2-week stay with estimated 3–4 events and 60% flagged-event conversion, each participant incurs ~5 PSG nights (3 scheduled + 2 escalation) consuming €380 per participant in enhanced equipment costs and technician labor (~€11.4K total year 2 across 35 participants). Deferring mid-stay PSG to event-triggered escalation only would reduce PSG nights to 3–4 per participant (€285–380) but risks missing spontaneous morphology variation unrelated to technician detection thresholds. Strategic: This lever trades diagnostic comprehensiveness (capturing spontaneous variation) against economic efficiency and technician workload, directly impacting whether cost containment allows phase-two expansion or mandates scope reduction.
options: Schedule full PSG on nights 1, 7, and 14 universally, supplemented by 48-hour escalation protocols only after technician-flagged events, preserving longitudinal morphology data and accepting €12–14K year 2 enhanced PSG cost.

Deploy PSG selectively: nightly actigraphy + EEG headband for all participants; full PSG only on nights 1 and 14 plus any technician-flagged event, deferring mid-stay PSG unless no events captured by day 10, reducing baseline PSG cost to €8–9K but accepting gaps in spontaneous variance.

Reserve full PSG exclusively for participant-reported or video-confirmed events detected by technicians in real time, relying on nightly dry-electrode EEG and mattress sensors as primary surveillance, cutting enhanced PSG to <€6K year 2 but risking loss of sub-clinical arousal and proprioceptive data.
levers.review_lever: Controls diagnostic depth vs. cost efficiency. Weakness: The options fail to consider whether dry-electrode EEG headbands and mattress sensors achieve sufficient quality for reliable event detection, potentially cascading into poor escalation precision.

levers.name: Manual Event Annotation Workflow and Dual-Scoring Throughput
levers.consequences: Immediate: Current plan specifies real-time technician annotation followed by two independent scorer review using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reporting; no timeline specified for second-scorer turnaround. Systemic: At 70% event-capture rate, year 2 enrollment of 35 participants, and ~3.5 events per participant, ~90 total annotated events require dual independent scoring; at 15 min per event-review by a qualified sleep technologist (€22/h contracted rate), second-scorer throughput demands 22.5 hours of external labor (~€494). If scorer throughput lags >4 weeks, algorithm training stalls and mid-year publications slip; if accelerated to <2 weeks, requires recruiting 2+ external raters and onboarding variance. Strategic: Annotation velocity directly gates semi-automated triage algorithm training (Aim 3) and publication timelines; insufficient inter-rater reliability signals scorer calibration failure and forces protocol revision, triggering month-18 review risk.
options: Contract two independent sleep medicine physicians within Bonn or University Hospital Bonn network to score all flagged events within 2–3 weeks of technician annotation, prioritizing diagnostic accuracy and publication-ready inter-rater metrics, accepting €1.2–1.5K year 2 cost.

Train the clinical psychologist and one research technician as secondary scorers using dual-rater workshops and periodic calibration sessions against recorded events, reducing external cost to ~€200 but relying on non-physician expertise and risking lower inter-rater agreement.

Develop a semi-automated pre-scoring algorithm (Aim 3 prototype) that flags putative events by month 6, reducing human scoring burden to high-confidence disagreements only, compressing dual-scoring to 5–8 min per event and enabling real-time algorithm feedback loops.
levers.review_lever: Controls annotation quality assurance vs. operational cost and timeline. Weakness: The options fail to consider how sleep medicine physicians in Bonn's network availability impacts turnaround, or whether outsourcing to national centers (e.g., Berlin sleep labs) introduces standardization gains at travel/logistic cost.

levers.name: Data Annotation Framework and Real-Time Technician Burden
levers.consequences: Immediate: Night technicians perform real-time annotation during live monitoring (flagging putative events, arousal timing, video/audio context) and incomplete AASM scoring, creating dual cognitive load alongside suite monitoring and safety checks. Systemic: Over 8-suite nights with 1–2 concurrent events and 3–5 min annotation per event, real-time scoring occupies ~12–15% of technician time per night; if event clustering coincides with PSG hookup duties or safety escalations, annotation completeness drops below 80%, requiring weekend catch-up labor estimated at 4–6 additional hours per technician per week (€80–120/week contingency cost). Strategic: Over-burdening technicians degrades annotation quality and safety vigilance; under-staffed annotation creates publish-quality backlogs and delays algorithm training, whereas over-investing in real-time scoring infrastructure (e.g., tablet-based AASM templates) accelerates scoring velocity but increases technician training and device management overhead.
options: Equip night technicians with tablet-based AASM scoring templates synchronized to EEG, enabling touchscreen event marking during live monitoring with automatic timestamp and metadata capture, front-loading annotation quality but requiring €8K software license and 2-day training per technician.

Shift all detailed AASM annotation to a daytime "annotation technician" role who reviews night recordings and technician flags within 24–48 h, freeing night staff for pure monitoring and safety, distributing scoring load across separate personnel but introducing 1–2 day scoring lag.

Implement a minimal real-time technician workflow (event flagging only, no full AASM markup) with structured annotation forms filled by technicians during post-event debriefing, deferring comprehensive AASM scoring to independent daytime raters, balancing technician burden and scoring consistency.
levers.review_lever: Controls real-time monitoring vigilance vs. annotation throughput and quality. Weakness: The options fail to consider how fatigue and competing priorities during night shifts degrade technician alertness for safety-critical decisions (emergency response, participant injury prevention) versus secondary annotation tasks.

levers.name: Semi-Automated Event Triage Tool Development and Validation Pathway
levers.consequences: Immediate: Aim 3 targets a machine-learning classifier that ranks putative parasomnia episodes by probability to reduce manual review burden; training data comes from dual-scored events and sensor streams (EEG, mattress, video motion). Systemic: By month 18, if ≥60 adjudicated events are available from year 1–early year 2 cohorts, a supervised model (e.g., random forest on EEG spectral features + mattress pressure derivatives) could achieve 75–85% sensitivity and 60–70% specificity against dual-scorer consensus; deployment to nightly sensor streams could reduce technician review from 100% of flagged events to ~30% high-confidence false positives, cutting annotation labor by ~60 hours year 2 (€1.3K). However, model performance plateaus if training set remains <100 events or if sensor noise (e.g., movement artifact in dry-electrode EEG) contaminates features. Strategic: A validated triage tool becomes a key publication and a phase-two funding justification, but overambitious claims (autonomous diagnosis) violate the banned-word constraint and invite regulatory skepticism; conservative benchmarking against human scorer agreement (not independent ground truth) accepts lower sensitivity but ensures publication credibility.
options: Use dual-scored events as training labels for a supervised classifier (random forest or logistic regression on raw EEG + mattress features), validated via cross-validation and reported sensitivity/specificity against human scorer consensus, accepting moderate performance and publishing results conservatively.

Develop an unsupervised clustering approach (e.g., t-SNE or UMAP on EEG embeddings) that identifies sub-phenotypes of parasomnia morphology without pre-defined labels, followed by supervised fine-tuning once cluster identity is adjudicated, enabling novel phenotype discovery but delaying practical triage deployment.

Train a convolutional neural network on time-frequency EEG spectrograms and video motion energy across all participants, optimized for event-likelihood ranking rather than binary classification, accepting higher computational cost and longer training time but potentially capturing non-linear patterns and enabling sensitivity tuning for operational deployment.
levers.review_lever: Controls scientific novelty vs. operational utility and publication conservatism. Weakness: The options fail to consider how multi-participant training on heterogeneous parasomnia morphologies may dilute individual-level signal, or whether personalized (per-participant) models offer better operational performance than population-level classifiers.

levers.name: Facility Utilization Economics and Capacity Expansion Gating
levers.consequences: Immediate: 8 active suites across a facility designed for 12 incurs ~€750K year 1 lease cost plus €120K utilities/maintenance fixed cost, amortized to €93.75K per active suite annually; at 70% occupancy over 2-week stays and 35 annual participants, average suite utilization is ~60 days/year per suite (capacity underuse). Systemic: Adding 4 suites requires minimal renovation cost (<€50K) but triggers 20% operational overhead increase (technician hours, data management, utilities) estimated at €25K additional year 2; breakeven on expanded capacity occurs at ~55 participants annually, achieved in year 2 only if recruitment ramps to 35+ and occupancy rises to 75%+ (high risk). If participant retention or event-capture drops, cost per adjudicated event rises from €4.2K (current projection) to €6.8K+, threatening grant-renewal funding efficiency. Strategic: Deferring 12-suite activation until year 3 preserves optionality and cost control; early expansion risks sunk capacity costs if recruitment underperforms, whereas conservative staffing and suite utilization through year 2 provides low-risk validation before scaling.
options: Activate all 12 suites in Q4 year 1, hiring a 4th technician and scaling operations to 50+ participants year 2, achieving economy of scale and phase-two funding momentum but accepting €30–50K higher fixed costs if enrollment shortfalls occur.

Maintain 8-suite operations through year 2 with a pre-planned 4-suite buildout starting Q3 year 2 (contingent on enrollment ≥30 and capture rate ≥65%), aligning capacity expansion with demonstrated demand and reducing financial exposure.

Reduce active suites to 6 through year 1, consolidating technician shifts and facilities management costs to €85K annually, investing savings into recruitment acceleration and event-capture optimization, accepting lower throughput but improving cost efficiency per participant.
levers.review_lever: Controls operational cost leverage vs. financial risk and recruitment flexibility. Weakness: The options fail to consider how staffing continuity and technician expertise develop more robustly with larger active cohorts, or whether smaller operations in year 2 may hamper publication output and competitiveness for phase-two funding relative to larger competing residential facilities.

responses.summary: The seven levers are strategically well-balanced, addressing the three core project tensions: ecological validity (recruitment gatekeeping, technician safety), scientific rigor (PSG scheduling, annotation quality, triage validation), and financial sustainability (staffing scalability, facility utilization). A critical strength is the progression from conservative to radical within each lever—e.g., staffing ranges from tight fixed rosters to flexible freelance models, and annotation spans full real-time overhead to night-shift minimalism. However, one crucial dimension is absent: participant retention and early-exit risk management. The plan assumes 70–80% event-capture success rates and 2–8 week stays, but provides no contingency for mid-stay withdrawals due to homesickness, safety discomfort, or perceived lack of progress. Add to Lever 2 (Staffing): "Implement a peer-mentor program pairing newly admitted participants with alumni who have completed stays, reducing mid-stay dropout via normalization and social support, compensating mentors at €10/h for two 1-h weekly calls, estimated cost €400–600 per cohort of 30, with target retention gain of 5–10 percentage points." This directly supports Aim 1 (operationally validate sustainability) and buffers event-capture yield variance.

responses.strategic_rationale: The Bonn parasomnia unit faces three interlocking tensions: (1) capturing rare, naturalistic events while managing false-alarm burden on night staff; (2) balancing cost-effective longitudinal recruitment against the clinical gatekeeping required to exclude confounds and ensure participant safety; (3) maintaining domestic ambiance to elicit authentic behavior while implementing institutional safety and data-governance protocols. The levers below target these fault lines by forcing concrete choices around sensor reliance, participant flow architecture, staffing skill-mix, data stewardship pathways, operational governance, financial sustainability, and algorithm ownership — each choice cascades into downstream resource, publication, and expansion consequences.

levers.name: Sensor Primacy Versus Human Technician Adjudication in Event Detection
levers.consequences: Immediate: Shifting reliance from technician visual/audio flagging toward mattress-sensor thresholds and automated alerts reduces nightly review cognitive load by an estimated 40–50% but increases false-positive escalation of ambiguous posture changes that mimic arousal. Systemic: Over a 3-year participant cohort, this trade-off between technician burnout prevention and annotation-rework cost accumulates; heavy sensor bias may require 10–15% more dual-scoring labor to validate borderline alerts, offsetting staffing relief. Strategic: A sensor-first posture locks the facility into expensive middleware tuning and vendor lock-in risk, while human-first primacy preserves methodological flexibility and publication credibility but risks technician fatigue and turnover.
options: Deploy mattress and infrared sensors as primary event triggers, with human technician review as secondary confirmation step, accepting 25–30% false-positive escalation to PSG in exchange for reduced nightly manual surveillance demands.

Maintain technician visual/audio observation as the gold standard for event flagging, with sensors serving as documentation and post-hoc pattern mining, accepting higher nightly staffing intensity but ensuring publication defensibility and lower annotation rework.

Institute a hybrid adaptive model where sensor confidence scores automatically gate technician review thresholds during high-activity nights (>5 potential events flagged), but revert to human-first gatekeeping during low-activity periods, requiring real-time threshold recalibration and periodic audits.
levers.review_lever: Controls technician workload vs. annotation accuracy and publication defensibility. Weakness: The options fail to consider the clinical safety implications of missed real events during high-burden nights or the participant experience of excessive false-alarm PSG escalations, which may drive study dropout.

levers.name: Inclusion Criterion Selectivity and Enrollment Velocity Trade-Off
levers.consequences: Immediate: Strict pre-admission screening (requiring prior documented episodes, bed-partner collateral, and exclusion-checklist adjudication) ensures high phenotypic homogeneity and reduces protocol deviations, but delays recruitment by an estimated 3–5 weeks per participant. Systemic: Over the 3-year enrollment target of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants, a 4-week average recruitment delay compresses usable data-collection time by approximately 15–20% relative to looser screening, potentially reducing captured event yield by 12–18% if stay duration is capped at 10 weeks. Strategic: Strict gatekeeping protects Aim 1 operational validation and publication rigor but risks undershooting the 40+ events threshold that triggers external program review at month 18, forcing either stay-duration extension (cost increase) or recruitment-criteria loosening (methodological compromise).
options: Enforce full pre-admission screening including prior polysomnography report review, structured clinician-led interviews, and mandatory collateral history, accepting 4–6 week recruitment cycles in exchange for phenotypic confidence and minimal protocol amendments.

Streamline intake to a brief telephone interview and self-report questionnaire, with post-admission polysomnography on night 1 serving as the confirmatory gate for stay continuation, reducing recruitment delay to 1–2 weeks but risking 10–15% participant exclusion after enrollment and associated data-collection inefficiency.

Implement a two-tiered pathway: routine cases admitted after phone screening alone, with enhanced pre-screening reserved for borderline or high-comorbidity referrals, creating a population mix of high-confidence and exploratory cohorts analyzed separately to balance velocity against phenotypic clarity.
levers.review_lever: Controls enrollment speed vs. phenotypic homogeneity and event-yield predictability. Weakness: The options fail to consider how recruitment momentum and early success stories (or failures) impact the credibility of referral pathways and institutional relationships with the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network.

levers.name: Matched Control Accrual and Baseline-Phenotyping Resource Allocation
levers.consequences: Immediate: Recruiting and hosting a matched control group (estimated 15–20 adults without parasomnia history) consumes approximately 10–15% of facility bed-nights and technician annotation capacity. Systemic: Control data enable rigorous sensor false-positive benchmarking and differential diagnosis learning (Aim 3 algorithm sensitivity/specificity), reducing semi-automated tool claims from 'promising indicators' to 'validated against normative baseline' — a shift worth 1–2 high-impact publications and approximately €200–300K in follow-on grant leverage. Strategic: Deferring controls to year 2 or outsourcing baseline collection to a separate institution saves immediate cash and accelerates case enrollment but surrenders first-publication priority and forces methodological dependence on external collaborators, weakening Bonn's hold on the algorithm IP narrative.
options: Recruit and host controls concurrently with case participants from month 1, distributing bed-night load and enabling real-time algorithm benchmarking at the cost of slower case accrual and compressed case stay duration.

Defer controls until year 2, after case phenotyping is established, allowing year-1 case enrollment to saturate available capacity and maximize within-person longitudinal depth, then conduct controls as a shorter burst study with dedicated summer staff resources.

Establish a remote control-monitoring arm where matched controls perform identical at-home monitoring using a subset of non-video sensors (EEG headband, mattress, microphone) without facility residence, reducing facility burden by 80% but limiting data quality and introducing recruitment attrition from non-residential inconvenience.
levers.review_lever: Controls facility capacity utilization vs. algorithm-validation rigor and publication impact. Weakness: The options fail to consider how control data latency affects interim algorithm iterations and whether year-1 case phenotyping sufficiently matures to define meaningful control-matching criteria without seeing control data first.

levers.name: RBD Protocol Integration Versus Standalone Neurologic Screening Separation
levers.consequences: Immediate: Incorporating REM sleep behavior disorder under a secondary, compartmentalized protocol (with dedicated neurologic screening per AASM guidance and distinct inclusion/exclusion criteria) adds an estimated €150–200K year-1 overhead for additional clinician review, separate consent documentation, and confound-management procedures. Systemic: RBD participants require specialized safety precautions (fall risk, injury thresholds) and distinct sleep-stage montage prioritization; integrating RBD into the main night-technician workflow increases procedural overhead and context-switching by approximately 20–30% per shift, likely pushing technician FTE demand from 3.0 to 3.3–3.4. Strategic: Launching RBD as a parallel subprogram signals scientific ambition and broadens publication scope (neurodegenerative implications, RBD-to-PD conversion patterns), but delays year-1 focus on core NREM parasomnia validation; alternatively, NREM-only year 1 with RBD deferred to year 2 reduces risk and concentrates resource but sacrifices early interdisciplinary visibility.
options: Operate NREM and RBD as fully integrated protocols within the same 8-suite facility, with unified night-technician training covering both condition-specific safety requirements and diagnostic polysomnographic montages, accepting higher staff cognitive load but maximizing facility versatility and publication breadth.

Launch NREM parasomnia only in year 1, with RBD explicitly deferred to a year-2 competitive pilot phase contingent on successful NREM methods validation and demonstrated technician competency, preserving focus and reducing year-1 overhead by €150K.

Establish RBD as a separate administrative protocol overseen by a partnering neurologist at University Hospital Bonn, with RBD participants using the facility's equipment and infrastructure but following distinct enrollment, consent, and safety procedures managed outside the core Bonn unit team, creating operational separation at the cost of administrative duplication.
levers.review_lever: Controls research scope and publication ambition vs. year-1 operational focus and technician cognitive workload. Weakness: The options fail to consider whether the facility's existing safety infrastructure and technician training adequately address RBD-specific fall and injury risk, or whether liability insurance and ethics-approval contingencies differ substantively between NREM and RBD cohorts.

levers.name: De-Identification and Data-Sharing Governance Pathway
levers.consequences: Immediate: Strict de-identification (removing video entirely, pseudonymizing physiological streams, restricting secondary use via data use agreements) ensures GDPR/ethics compliance and participant privacy protection but prevents post-hoc qualitative validation of sensor-flagged events against video ground truth and limits the richness of phenotypic analysis (e.g., behavioral context, injury mechanism reconstruction). Systemic: A permissive sharing policy (physiological + de-identified video metadata, with tiered access via formal DUA process) accelerates algorithm benchmarking and attracts secondary-analysis collaborators, potentially yielding 1–2 additional high-impact publications per year but increases participant privacy concerns and requires ongoing data governance oversight (estimated €40–60K year-1 compliance labor). Strategic: Conservative de-identification maximizes participant trust and reduces regulatory exposure but cedes competitive advantage in algorithm development to institutions with less-constrained datasets; permissive sharing positions Bonn as a central hub but invites mission creep and de-identification breach risk.
options: Deposit only EEG, movement, and environmental time-series data in public repositories under BIDS-NIFTI standards, with video confined to local secure storage accessible only to Bonn team members under strict ethics oversight, accepting limited external algorithm collaboration but preserving participant privacy maximally.

Implement a tiered-access model: physiological data and de-identified video metadata (e.g., frame-level motion scores, event timestamps) shared under formal DUA with academic institutions, while raw video remains on-premises for Bonn-internal validation only, balancing transparency and secondary-use opportunity against privacy.

Establish a federated-learning framework where external collaborators submit algorithm code to the Bonn data server for local execution and return only aggregate accuracy metrics and aggregate embeddings, rather than exporting raw data, eliminating traditional data export but requiring substantial middleware investment (€80–120K development).
levers.review_lever: Controls participant privacy and regulatory compliance vs. secondary-use scientific leverage and algorithm external validation. Weakness: The options fail to consider heterogeneous participant willingness to share video (some may consent, others refuse), and whether ethical approval permits selective tiering based on individual consent preference or requires uniform de-identification across all participants.

levers.name: Algorithm Ownership and Pharma-Partnership IP Alignment
levers.consequences: Immediate: Securing an optional pharmaceutical partnership (estimated €400–500K additional funding) introduces contractual IP provisions; a default clause offering pharma a 30-day pre-publication comment window is lightweight, but pharma pressure for exclusive access to algorithm code or training data during the 3-year window constrains Bonn's publication timeline and secondary-collaboration options. Systemic: Pharma partnership accelerates algorithm validation work (dedicated computational resources, industry benchmark datasets) and could yield a high-impact algorithm validation paper by month 24, but any exclusive data/code restrictions delay open-source release by an estimated 6–12 months, reducing first-mover advantage in community adoption and follow-on method papers. Strategic: Accepting pharma funding without strict IP guardrails risks subordinating academic publication priorities to industry timelines; conversely, rejecting industry co-funding reduces budget flexibility (forcing more conservative year-2 scope or RBD deferral) but preserves unfettered publication freedom and positions Bonn as an independent data steward.
options: Pursue pharma funding with a contractual ceiling on exclusivity: 30-day pre-publication review window only, no exclusive data access, and immediate open-source algorithm release upon publication, accepting a modest funding amount (€250–300K) if full-scale pharma commitment is unavailable.

Decline pharma funding entirely, relying solely on DFG and University of Bonn resources, maintaining absolute publication autonomy and algorithm open-source status from month 12 onward, accepting tighter year-2 and year-3 budgets and possible scope reduction (e.g., RBD deferral).

Negotiate a full pharma partnership with a formal technology-development agreement: pharma receives 18-month exclusive algorithm access and co-author visibility on methods papers, in exchange for €500K funding and dedicated pharma computational resources, accepting delayed public release but securing substantial capital and industry-grade validation work.
levers.review_lever: Controls academic independence and publication velocity vs. computational resources and algorithm validation rigor. Weakness: The options fail to consider the reputational and recruitment risk if pharma involvement becomes public and influences clinician referrals or participant enrollment, or whether DFG and University of Bonn stakeholders have institutional preferences about pharma partnerships.

levers.name: Residential Facility Expansion Trigger and Capacity-Scaling Decision Point
levers.consequences: Immediate: Deferring expansion from 8 to 12 suites until after month 18–24 validation (current plan) requires demonstrable success: ≥25 enrolled participants, ≥40 captured events, and manageable technician and annotation workload on the current 8-suite model. Systemic: A month-18 go/no-go expansion decision compounds with recruitment velocity and event-yield assumptions; if case accrual lags (e.g., only 18 participants by month 18), expanding to 12 suites commits an additional €300–400K capital (4 new suites, renovation, equipment) without demand certainty, risking stranded infrastructure and budget pressure. Strategic: Immediate expansion to 12 suites at launch (an additional €300K upfront) accelerates case enrollment and event capture, shortening the path to Aim 2 phenotyping conclusions and hypothesis-generating publications, but front-loads risk if recruitment proves slower or false-alarm rates force extensive facility repairs, and reduces DFG's flexibility to reallocate funds if early operational issues arise.
options: Maintain 8-suite operations through month 20, with a formal month-18 external-board review decision on 12-suite expansion contingent on meeting enrollment and event-capture targets, preserving budget optionality and risk mitigation at the cost of slower case throughput if demand is high.

Construct and equip 4 additional suites in parallel with year-1 operations (phased renovation starting month 3), maintaining year-1 staffing at 3.0 FTE but expanding to 4.0–4.2 FTE in year 2, accepting frontend capital commitment but ensuring capacity-ready infrastructure if recruitment accelerates.

Lease a second adjacent property in the Bonn neighborhood starting year 2, creating a geographically distributed 8 + 8 suite model with shared support services and cross-facility technician coverage, enabling 60%+ capacity scaling without on-site construction but introducing operational complexity and geographic dispersion of participant safety oversight.
levers.review_lever: Controls upfront capital commitment and budget optionality vs. capacity readiness and enrollment throughput potential. Weakness: The options fail to consider how facility reputation and word-of-mouth recruitment momentum among referring clinicians may suddenly accelerate demand after the first 6 months, stranding the 8-suite model if expansion cannot be fast-tracked, or whether distributed multi-site operations degrade data quality and technician training coherence.

responses.summary: The seven levers balance immediate operational tensions (sensor vs. human judgment, enrollment speed vs. phenotypic rigor, facility utilization vs. control-group validation) against strategic sustainability (RBD scope, IP governance, expansion staging). Strengths: levers challenge core assumptions (sensor primacy, recruitment gatekeeping, data openness) and force explicit cost/benefit trade-offs with quantified cascades. Weakness: The portfolio under-addresses participant retention and stay-duration variability — if 20–30% of admitted cases produce zero events and stay-extension options (capped at 10 weeks) backfill capacity unpredictably, the facility's throughput math breaks. Recommendation: Add an eighth lever: 'Zero-Event Participant Disposition and Stay-Duration Extension Strategy,' offering options to (1) rigidly enforce 2-week baseline + 2-week extension cap and discharge unproductive cases, (2) implement a dynamic stay-extension review at week 6 with PI discretion to extend up to 10 weeks based on covert event indicators (video-flagged movements, family reports), or (3) create a 'prospective overnight event monitoring' extension track where zero-event participants transition to randomized-schedule 1–2 night revisits over months 3–6 post-discharge at reduced cost, maintaining engagement without tying up active suite-nights.

responses.strategic_rationale: This early-stage research facility faces acute trade-offs between scientific rigor (longitudinal event capture, dual-scored adjudication) and operational sustainability (8-suite staffing, annotation throughput, cost containment). The levers below target the core tensions: whether to prioritize participant volume or event yield; how aggressively to automate triage versus maintain human oversight; whether to embed pharmaceutical partnerships that bring revenue but risk publication autonomy; how to scale physical capacity without exceeding safe staffing ratios; and whether to pursue licensing partnerships for sensor hardware or maintain full in-house control. These levers are critical because they directly determine whether the 24-month validation milestone is achievable within the €3.8M budget and whether the facility can sustain beyond the pilot without external revenue streams.

levers.name: Dual-Scorer Reliability Threshold and Adjudication Consensus Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Setting inter-rater agreement targets (e.g., Cohen's kappa ≥0.70 vs. ≥0.85) determines how frequently events are re-scored or arbitrated by a third clinician. Systemic: Tighter thresholds reduce disputed events by an estimated 15–25% but increase scoring labor by approximately 30–40 hours per month, consuming 10–15% of the annual independent-scoring budget (~€15K–€25K annually). Strategic: High consensus standards strengthen publication credibility and algorithmic training data quality, but lenient standards accelerate time-to-analysis and reduce the risk of recruitment stalling if event yield is lower than projected.
options: Adopt a two-tiered adjudication protocol in which events below the kappa threshold automatically escalate to PI review, with consensus required before inclusion in phenotyping analysis, prioritizing methodological rigor over speed.

Implement a pragmatic consensus rule in which disagreements between the two primary scorers are resolved by majority-rule category assignment (e.g., if one scorer flags a confusional arousal and the other flags a sleep terror, assign the more conservative label) without third-party arbitration, balancing cost and timeline.

Establish a machine-assisted disambiguation workflow in which disputed events are re-reviewed jointly by both scorers using side-by-side video and signal playback with an algorithm-generated confidence score displayed, aiming to achieve consensus without escalation in 80% of cases.
levers.review_lever: Controls methodological rigor (stringent consensus) vs. operational feasibility (pragmatic dispute resolution). Weakness: The options fail to consider how partial arousal events or ambiguous polysomnographic morphology (e.g., slow-wave sleep with movement) will be classified, which may inflate disagreement rates independent of scorer training quality.

levers.name: Matched Control Recruitment Intensity and Baseline Phenotype Depth
levers.consequences: Immediate: Recruiting matched controls (age, sex, BMI, sleep-disorder-free status confirmed by screening questionnaire) from hospital employee pools, university staff, and community volunteers requires dedicated coordinator time for outreach and screening. Systemic: A 1:1 matched control-to-case ratio demands ~50–70 additional overnight stays (~10,000–14,000 sensor-monitoring nights) beyond the parasomnia cohort, increasing facility occupancy by 25–35%, but provides robust benchmark data for sensor false-positive rates and normative arousal baselines, reducing algorithmic training bias by an estimated 20–30%. Strategic: Comprehensive control recruitment strengthens the event-triage tool's specificity and the paper's methodological credibility, but delays phenotyping analysis by 6–12 months and strains night-technician capacity if controls are not staggered across years two and three.
options: Prioritize complete 1:1 matching throughout the 3-year study, with matched controls recruited in parallel to parasomnia participants and distributed evenly across years two and three, ensuring every case has a contemporaneous control baseline for cross-validation.

Implement a deferred matching strategy in which controls are recruited en masse in year two after parasomnia cohort enrollment stabilizes, allowing year-one operations to focus on case accrual and facility optimization before committing control-recruitment resources.

Adopt a minimal-matching approach targeting 1:2 case-to-control ratio using historical normative polysomnography data from the Bonn hospital's sleep clinic archive rather than new overnight stays, supplemented by only 15–20 new controls to validate sensor accuracy.
levers.review_lever: Controls scientific rigor (contemporaneous 1:1 matched controls) vs. operational resource concentration (deferred or minimal matching). Weakness: The options fail to address whether controls should undergo identical enhanced-night PSG protocols and escalation-night procedures or whether a simpler single-night monitoring schema would suffice, which materially affects staffing load and comparability.

levers.name: Pharmaceutical Partnership Sequencing and Publication Autonomy Safeguards
levers.consequences: Immediate: Early-stage pharma partnership engagement (months 3–6) secures co-funding (~€400K–€600K annually) and drug-company interest in phenotyping results, but introduces pre-publication review periods and potential scope redirection toward drug-relevant sub-phenotypes. Systemic: A 30-day pre-publication comment window without veto rights preserves the team's autonomy but could delay major publications by 1–3 months; conversely, deferring partnership until year two allows methodological independence in early publications but risks losing pharma interest if preliminary findings are already public, reducing supplementary funding by an estimated 40–50%. Strategic: Early partnership de-risks the year-three budget contingency and accelerates infrastructure development, but late partnership maximizes publication independence and recruitment flexibility, though it increases reliance on DFG/university funding renewal.
options: Initiate partnership discussions immediately post-ethics approval (month 4–5) with a pharma company's sleep medicine division, negotiate a sponsored-research agreement with explicit carve-outs for publication timeline and scope, and deploy partnership funding to accelerate sensor validation and technician hiring in year one.

Delay partnership engagement until month 18 (mid-year two) once preliminary longitudinal data, event-capture rates, and early algorithmic results are available, allowing the academic team to publish foundational methods papers independently and then invite pharma interest from a position of demonstrated capability.

Pursue a lightweight industry-collaboration model in which equipment vendors (e.g., dry-electrode EEG headband manufacturers, mattress-sensor companies) provide in-kind hardware and co-authorship on methods papers in exchange for field validation data, avoiding direct pharma funding and its governance overhead.
levers.review_lever: Controls publication autonomy (no pharma pre-publication review) vs. financial de-risking (pharma co-funding accelerates year-three contingency coverage). Weakness: The options fail to specify how recruitment messaging, inclusion-criterion scope, or long-term follow-up study design might shift if a pharma partner has strategic interest in specific parasomnia phenotypes or pharmaceutical endpoints.

levers.name: Sensor Hardware Procurement Model and Sensor Reliability Benchmarking
levers.consequences: Immediate: Procuring dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, and infrared cameras either as a single integrated platform (e.g., one vendor suite) or as best-of-class components from multiple suppliers with custom middleware. Systemic: Single-vendor integration reduces software debugging and inter-device calibration time by an estimated 25–30% and simplifies vendor support, but locks the facility into one manufacturer's product roadmap and pricing; multi-vendor modular procurement provides technology flexibility and lowers per-unit cost by 10–15% but demands 200–300 additional engineering hours (€15K–€25K) for middleware development and ongoing cross-vendor troubleshooting. Strategic: Single-vendor standardization accelerates the pilot-phase validation timeline and reduces startup risk, while multi-vendor modularity preserves long-term technology agility and prevents vendor lock-in, critical if a sensor platform is discontinued or major software flaws emerge.
options: Select a single integrated sensor platform (e.g., a vendor offering EEG headband, mattress pressure sensors, and video recording with unified cloud synchronization) with a 3-year commercial license, prioritizing turnkey reliability and vendor technical support over absolute cost minimization.

Assemble a modular sensor architecture from leading point-solutions in each category (premium dry-EEG headband, industrial-grade pressure-mat sensors, clinical-standard infrared camera, open-source time-synchronization middleware) with a data engineer dedicated to middleware maintenance, accepting higher upfront engineering cost for maximum long-term flexibility.

Establish a hardware co-development partnership with two sensor manufacturers in which the facility provides design feedback, field validation data, and early-adopter deployment in exchange for discounted hardware and embedded engineering support, positioning Bonn as a reference site for product maturation.
levers.review_lever: Controls vendor lock-in risk (modular multi-vendor) vs. operational simplicity (single integrated platform). Weakness: The options fail to address how sensor failures, firmware updates, or unexpected incompatibilities will be diagnosed and resolved during nights when the facility is fully occupied, or how backup hardware will be stockpiled and maintained.

levers.name: Residential Environment Authenticity Versus Clinical Monitoring Standardization
levers.consequences: Immediate: Balancing 'domestic feel' (soft lighting, resident-style furnishings, unsupervised common areas, flexible bedtimes) against standardized clinical conditions (standardized room temperature 20–22°C, identical bedding, noise thresholds, coordinated lighting schedules) determines participant naturalness and sensor artifact. Systemic: Prioritizing authenticity increases homesickness-related early withdrawals and participant-reported sleep quality variability by an estimated 10–20%, but boosts ecological validity and captures sleep behavior closer to home conditions; conversely, strict standardization reduces withdrawal risk by 5–10% and improves signal consistency but may suppress parasomnia expression if the environment is perceived as too clinical, potentially reducing event yield by 15–25%. Strategic: High authenticity supports Aim 2 (longitudinal phenotyping) by capturing naturalistic within-person triggers and clustering patterns, while standardization strengthens Aim 3 (event-triage algorithm training) by reducing sensor noise and confounding environmental variation.
options: Design sleep suites with hotel-style comfort (blackout-capable but aesthetic window dressing, personal climate controls in the 18–24°C range, individualized bedding preferences with provided hypoallergenic options, soft ambient lighting with dimmer controls) and common areas mimicking a boutique residential setting, accepting 15–20% higher environmental variability in exchange for reduced study dropout.

Establish standardized clinical suite conditions with objective environmental monitoring (±1°C temperature tolerance, sound levels <40 dB during sleep, LED light spectrum matched to circadian research standards, hospital-grade bedding and linens) and mandatory participant briefing on sleep hygiene, prioritizing algorithm-training data quality over comfort-driven authenticity.

Implement a two-phase environment protocol in which participants undergo an initial 3-night acclimatization stay in a comfort-prioritized residential suite (weeks 1–2), then transition to a standardized clinical monitoring suite for weeks 3–8, allowing naturalistic baseline assessment followed by controlled phenotyping capture.
levers.review_lever: Controls naturalistic event expression (comfort-prioritized environment) vs. sensor-data consistency (standardized clinical conditions). Weakness: The options fail to consider how individual circadian preference (chronotype), prior hospitalization anxiety, or unfamiliar bed-sharing arrangements with roommates will affect parasomnia expression independent of room environment, limiting the validity of environmental standardization.

levers.name: Night-Technician Scope Expansion and Real-Time Annotation Authority
levers.consequences: Immediate: Defining whether night technicians perform real-time event annotation using AASM criteria (flagging suspected episodes, recording timestamp and morphology live) or instead maintain passive observation logs deferred to daytime scorers. Systemic: Real-time AASM annotation by technicians requires 6–8 weeks of additional training (€3K–€5K cost and hiring delay) and increases nightly documentation time by 30–40 minutes per event, but accelerates escalation-night triggering and may reduce post-hoc scoring ambiguity by 10–20% because technician context is captured contemporaneously; passive logging reduces technician cognitive load and training burden but introduces a 12–24 hour lag before events are formally categorized, risking sleep-state context loss and escalation delays. Strategic: Real-time annotation authority empowers technicians as front-line clinicians and improves event-response speed, but may introduce systematic bias if technician training quality is heterogeneous, while passive observation maintains rigor but concentrates scoring burden on daytime staff and may miss subtle event recurrences requiring urgent escalation.
options: Invest in comprehensive AASM-criteria training for all night technicians (8-week program delivered by a board-certified sleep specialist, with competency testing and annual retraining) and authorize real-time event flagging with technician-recorded morphology descriptions, enabling same-night escalation decisions and contextual annotation.

Maintain night technicians as objective monitors who record video timestamps and physiological signal excerpts without categorical event assignment, with all AASM classification and escalation decisions deferred to the next-morning PI review, protecting scoring independence but accepting the 12–24 hour lag.

Establish a hybrid protocol in which technicians receive brief 2-week training on parasomnia-specific red flags (e.g., patient climbing out of bed, audible vocalization, thrashing leg movement) and are authorized to flag 'high-probability events' for immediate video review by an on-call clinician, stopping short of formal AASM diagnosis.
levers.review_lever: Controls technician autonomy and event-response speed (real-time AASM annotation) vs. scoring rigor and consistency (deferred daytime classification). Weakness: The options fail to address how technician fatigue during the 8-hour shift will degrade event detection sensitivity, or how to maintain annotation quality if technicians work non-overlapping week-long rotations and lose continuity of individual participant behavior.

levers.name: Algorithm Development Governance and External Validation Pathway
levers.consequences: Immediate: Developing the semi-automated event-triage tool either in-house using dual-scorer ground truth (slower, lower risk of premature deployment, full academic control) or in partnership with a machine-learning-focused academic or industry lab (faster, higher external credibility risk if partner prioritizes generalization over parasomnia specificity). Systemic: In-house development by the postdoctoral computational neuroscientist takes approximately 12–15 months and delays algorithm benchmarking to month 24, but ensures full intellectual ownership and alignment with AASM criteria; external partnership can accelerate algorithm readiness to month 18–20 but introduces 15–20% risk of algorithm drift toward pharmaceutical or diagnostic-market objectives misaligned with the study's research aims, and requires formal data-sharing and IP agreements (€10K–€20K legal cost). Strategic: In-house development positions the team for follow-on grant funding and licensing partnerships, while external partnership leverages specialized ML expertise and accelerates publication, but may compromise the facility's independence and create conflicts with publication timelines if the partner has commercial interests.
options: Retain full in-house algorithm development led by the postdoctoral computational neuroscientist using dual-scored parasomnia events as ground truth, publish the algorithm and benchmarking results as an open-science repository with code and weights shared under a CC-BY license, and pursue licensing only after peer-reviewed validation is complete.

Establish a structured collaboration with a sleep-informatics research group at another European university (e.g., Leiden, Amsterdam, or Zürich) with complementary expertise in physiological signal processing, jointly own the algorithm intellectual property with pre-agreed publication timeline and co-authorship, and cross-validate on both Bonn and partner cohorts.

Engage a specialized medical-AI consultancy to develop the triage algorithm using federated learning, in which the algorithm learns from Bonn data without raw physiological signals leaving the facility, with the consultancy retaining algorithm architecture IP but the facility retaining full control of parasomnia-specific models and publication rights.
levers.review_lever: Controls intellectual ownership and publication autonomy (in-house development) vs. algorithmic sophistication and timeline acceleration (external partnership). Weakness: The options fail to specify how the algorithm will be prospectively validated on new incoming participants in real-time, or whether external validation cohorts (e.g., other European sleep centers) will be solicited, which is critical for assessing generalization and supporting a future multi-center expansion grant.

responses.summary: These seven levers are well-balanced and directly address the facility's core trade-offs: scientific rigor versus timeline efficiency (Levers 1, 3, 7), operational sustainability versus methodological depth (Levers 2, 5, 6), and technology risk versus flexibility (Lever 4). However, a critical missing dimension is participant retention and long-term follow-up strategy. The plan mentions stays up to 10 weeks and extended admissions for zero-event cases, but does not address whether participants will be invited back for repeat admissions in years two or three to capture longitudinal within-person patterns (e.g., seasonal variation, medication effects, disease progression). Add the lever: 'Repeat-Admission Cohort Design and Multi-Year Longitudinal Follow-Up Intensity' with options spanning (1) no repeats, single-admission design; (2) invitation-based optional repeats in year three for willing participants; (3) structured mandatory second admission 6–12 months post-discharge for a subset of high-event participants, to disambiguate trait versus state factors in parasomnia expression—this directly affects recruitment messaging, participant burden, and Aim 2's longitudinal characterization fidelity.

levers.name: Recruitment Pathway Intensity and Clinical Gatekeeping
levers.consequences: Immediate: Stricter pre-admission diagnostic confirmation (polysomnography + collateral history + PI adjudication) reduces enrollment velocity but eliminates marginal cases and nocturnal seizure mimics that would consume technician time on non-parasomnia events. Systemic: A 15–20% reduction in false-inclusion diagnoses lowers unproductive admission days by an estimated 8–12 days per cohort of 30 participants, freeing ~€2.4K in participant compensation and technician overtime for higher-yield recruitment channels. Strategic: Early diagnostic rigor builds methodological credibility for Aim 2 phenotyping publications but risks missing the month-18 enrollment target (25+ participants) if referral networks perceive barriers too high, forcing protocol relaxation midway.
options: Require documented polysomnography from referring clinician or conduct screening PSG for all pre-admission candidates, with PI final adjudication on ICSD-3 diagnostic criteria and seizure-mimicry exclusion, accepting 4–6 week intake delays.

Partner with University Hospital Bonn's sleep clinic for fast-track referral of clinically confirmed NREM parasomnia patients already in treatment, supplemented by regional neurology networks, balancing speed with selectivity.

Implement rapid telephone-based diagnostic interview by the clinical psychologist during initial contact, fast-tracking low-risk candidates to admission while reserving formal PSG for ambiguous cases, accelerating enrollment but increasing post-hoc exclusion risk.
levers.review: Controls diagnostic certainty vs. recruitment velocity. Weakness: The options fail to consider how pre-admission dropout (participants who decline after detailed screening) scales with pathway stringency, potentially offsetting savings in unproductive admissions.

levers.name: Night Technician Staffing and Event-Response Scalability
levers.consequences: Immediate: Current 3-technician roster covers 8 active suites with 1.33 technicians per night on rotating shifts, allowing real-time annotation and event escalation; each technician performs 1–2 enhanced PSG hookups and monitors ~2.7 suites simultaneously during sleep. Systemic: If event frequency per participant averages 3 episodes per 2-week stay (consistent with inclusion criterion of ≥2/month), and escalation nights demand 45 min per flagged event, technician overtime scales nonlinearly; at 70% event-capture rate across 35 participants in year 2, estimated overtime burden rises to 380 hours (€5.7K additional cost) if staffing remains static, or forces deprioritization of real-time scoring. Strategic: Adding a 4th rotating technician (+€35K year 2) sustains safe coverage margins and timely annotation through 12-suite expansion, but delays cost-neutral single-shift operations; deferring 4th hire risks missed events and degraded data quality if event clustering coincides with vacation or illness.
options: Maintain 3-technician roster through year 2 with external freelance polysomnography technicians contracted on-demand for escalation nights, capping fixed overhead while accepting scheduling unpredictability and onboarding variability.

Hire a 4th technician on a fixed overnight contract starting Q2 year 2, aligned with projected enrollment ramp, distributing suite load to <2 per technician and building operational redundancy before 12-suite capacity expansion.

Implement a hybrid model where lead technicians train and supervise 2–3 rotating per-diem staff rotating on 2–3 night blocks per week, creating a tiered wage structure (reduces per-person cost) but introducing training overhead and quality inconsistency.
levers.review: Controls labor cost vs. operational safety margin. Weakness: The options fail to consider how technician fatigue and annotation accuracy degrade under sustained overtime, potentially biasing event-triage algorithm training on low-quality scored data.

levers.name: Tiered PSG Scheduling and Targeted Escalation Protocol
levers.consequences: Immediate: Current plan schedules full PSG montage on admission night, mid-stay (day 10–14), and discharge; technician-flagged events trigger unscheduled escalation nights within 48 h. Systemic: Over a 2-week stay with estimated 3–4 events and 60% flagged-event conversion, each participant incurs ~5 PSG nights (3 scheduled + 2 escalation) consuming €380 per participant in enhanced equipment costs and technician labor (~€11.4K total year 2 across 35 participants). Deferring mid-stay PSG to event-triggered escalation only would reduce PSG nights to 3–4 per participant (€285–380) but risks missing spontaneous morphology variation unrelated to technician detection thresholds. Strategic: This lever trades diagnostic comprehensiveness (capturing spontaneous variation) against economic efficiency and technician workload, directly impacting whether cost containment allows phase-two expansion or mandates scope reduction.
options: Schedule full PSG on nights 1, 7, and 14 universally, supplemented by 48-hour escalation protocols only after technician-flagged events, preserving longitudinal morphology data and accepting €12–14K year 2 enhanced PSG cost.

Deploy PSG selectively: nightly actigraphy + EEG headband for all participants; full PSG only on nights 1 and 14 plus any technician-flagged event, deferring mid-stay PSG unless no events captured by day 10, reducing baseline PSG cost to €8–9K but accepting gaps in spontaneous variance.

Reserve full PSG exclusively for participant-reported or video-confirmed events detected by technicians in real time, relying on nightly dry-electrode EEG and mattress sensors as primary surveillance, cutting enhanced PSG to <€6K year 2 but risking loss of sub-clinical arousal and proprioceptive data.
levers.review: Controls diagnostic depth vs. cost efficiency. Weakness: The options fail to consider whether dry-electrode EEG headbands and mattress sensors achieve sufficient quality for reliable event detection, potentially cascading into poor escalation precision.

levers.name: Manual Event Annotation Workflow and Dual-Scoring Throughput
levers.consequences: Immediate: Current plan specifies real-time technician annotation followed by two independent scorer review using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reporting; no timeline specified for second-scorer turnaround. Systemic: At 70% event-capture rate, year 2 enrollment of 35 participants, and ~3.5 events per participant, ~90 total annotated events require dual independent scoring; at 15 min per event-review by a qualified sleep technologist (€22/h contracted rate), second-scorer throughput demands 22.5 hours of external labor (~€494). If scorer throughput lags >4 weeks, algorithm training stalls and mid-year publications slip; if accelerated to <2 weeks, requires recruiting 2+ external raters and onboarding variance. Strategic: Annotation velocity directly gates semi-automated triage algorithm training (Aim 3) and publication timelines; insufficient inter-rater reliability signals scorer calibration failure and forces protocol revision, triggering month-18 review risk.
options: Contract two independent sleep medicine physicians within Bonn or University Hospital Bonn network to score all flagged events within 2–3 weeks of technician annotation, prioritizing diagnostic accuracy and publication-ready inter-rater metrics, accepting €1.2–1.5K year 2 cost.

Train the clinical psychologist and one research technician as secondary scorers using dual-rater workshops and periodic calibration sessions against recorded events, reducing external cost to ~€200 but relying on non-physician expertise and risking lower inter-rater agreement.

Develop a semi-automated pre-scoring algorithm (Aim 3 prototype) that flags putative events by month 6, reducing human scoring burden to high-confidence disagreements only, compressing dual-scoring to 5–8 min per event and enabling real-time algorithm feedback loops.
levers.review: Controls annotation quality assurance vs. operational cost and timeline. Weakness: The options fail to consider how sleep medicine physicians in Bonn's network availability impacts turnaround, or whether outsourcing to national centers (e.g., Berlin sleep labs) introduces standardization gains at travel/logistic cost.

levers.name: Data Annotation Framework and Real-Time Technician Burden
levers.consequences: Immediate: Night technicians perform real-time annotation during live monitoring (flagging putative events, arousal timing, video/audio context) and incomplete AASM scoring, creating dual cognitive load alongside suite monitoring and safety checks. Systemic: Over 8-suite nights with 1–2 concurrent events and 3–5 min annotation per event, real-time scoring occupies ~12–15% of technician time per night; if event clustering coincides with PSG hookup duties or safety escalations, annotation completeness drops below 80%, requiring weekend catch-up labor estimated at 4–6 additional hours per technician per week (€80–120/week contingency cost). Strategic: Over-burdening technicians degrades annotation quality and safety vigilance; under-staffed annotation creates publish-quality backlogs and delays algorithm training, whereas over-investing in real-time scoring infrastructure (e.g., tablet-based AASM templates) accelerates scoring velocity but increases technician training and device management overhead.
options: Equip night technicians with tablet-based AASM scoring templates synchronized to EEG, enabling touchscreen event marking during live monitoring with automatic timestamp and metadata capture, front-loading annotation quality but requiring €8K software license and 2-day training per technician.

Shift all detailed AASM annotation to a daytime "annotation technician" role who reviews night recordings and technician flags within 24–48 h, freeing night staff for pure monitoring and safety, distributing scoring load across separate personnel but introducing 1–2 day scoring lag.

Implement a minimal real-time technician workflow (event flagging only, no full AASM markup) with structured annotation forms filled by technicians during post-event debriefing, deferring comprehensive AASM scoring to independent daytime raters, balancing technician burden and scoring consistency.
levers.review: Controls real-time monitoring vigilance vs. annotation throughput and quality. Weakness: The options fail to consider how fatigue and competing priorities during night shifts degrade technician alertness for safety-critical decisions (emergency response, participant injury prevention) versus secondary annotation tasks.

levers.name: Semi-Automated Event Triage Tool Development and Validation Pathway
levers.consequences: Immediate: Aim 3 targets a machine-learning classifier that ranks putative parasomnia episodes by probability to reduce manual review burden; training data comes from dual-scored events and sensor streams (EEG, mattress, video motion). Systemic: By month 18, if ≥60 adjudicated events are available from year 1–early year 2 cohorts, a supervised model (e.g., random forest on EEG spectral features + mattress pressure derivatives) could achieve 75–85% sensitivity and 60–70% specificity against dual-scorer consensus; deployment to nightly sensor streams could reduce technician review from 100% of flagged events to ~30% high-confidence false positives, cutting annotation labor by ~60 hours year 2 (€1.3K). However, model performance plateaus if training set remains <100 events or if sensor noise (e.g., movement artifact in dry-electrode EEG) contaminates features. Strategic: A validated triage tool becomes a key publication and a phase-two funding justification, but overambitious claims (autonomous diagnosis) violate the banned-word constraint and invite regulatory skepticism; conservative benchmarking against human scorer agreement (not independent ground truth) accepts lower sensitivity but ensures publication credibility.
options: Use dual-scored events as training labels for a supervised classifier (random forest or logistic regression on raw EEG + mattress features), validated via cross-validation and reported sensitivity/specificity against human scorer consensus, accepting moderate performance and publishing results conservatively.

Develop an unsupervised clustering approach (e.g., t-SNE or UMAP on EEG embeddings) that identifies sub-phenotypes of parasomnia morphology without pre-defined labels, followed by supervised fine-tuning once cluster identity is adjudicated, enabling novel phenotype discovery but delaying practical triage deployment.

Train a convolutional neural network on time-frequency EEG spectrograms and video motion energy across all participants, optimized for event-likelihood ranking rather than binary classification, accepting higher computational cost and longer training time but potentially capturing non-linear patterns and enabling sensitivity tuning for operational deployment.
levers.review: Controls scientific novelty vs. operational utility and publication conservatism. Weakness: The options fail to consider how multi-participant training on heterogeneous parasomnia morphologies may dilute individual-level signal, or whether personalized (per-participant) models offer better operational performance than population-level classifiers.

levers.name: Facility Utilization Economics and Capacity Expansion Gating
levers.consequences: Immediate: 8 active suites across a facility designed for 12 incurs ~€750K year 1 lease cost plus €120K utilities/maintenance fixed cost, amortized to €93.75K per active suite annually; at 70% occupancy over 2-week stays and 35 annual participants, average suite utilization is ~60 days/year per suite (capacity underuse). Systemic: Adding 4 suites requires minimal renovation cost (<€50K) but triggers 20% operational overhead increase (technician hours, data management, utilities) estimated at €25K additional year 2; breakeven on expanded capacity occurs at ~55 participants annually, achieved in year 2 only if recruitment ramps to 35+ and occupancy rises to 75%+ (high risk). If participant retention or event-capture drops, cost per adjudicated event rises from €4.2K (current projection) to €6.8K+, threatening grant-renewal funding efficiency. Strategic: Deferring 12-suite activation until year 3 preserves optionality and cost control; early expansion risks sunk capacity costs if recruitment underperforms, whereas conservative staffing and suite utilization through year 2 provides low-risk validation before scaling.
options: Activate all 12 suites in Q4 year 1, hiring a 4th technician and scaling operations to 50+ participants year 2, achieving economy of scale and phase-two funding momentum but accepting €30–50K higher fixed costs if enrollment shortfalls occur.

Maintain 8-suite operations through year 2 with a pre-planned 4-suite buildout starting Q3 year 2 (contingent on enrollment ≥30 and capture rate ≥65%), aligning capacity expansion with demonstrated demand and reducing financial exposure.

Reduce active suites to 6 through year 1, consolidating technician shifts and facilities management costs to €85K annually, investing savings into recruitment acceleration and event-capture optimization, accepting lower throughput but improving cost efficiency per participant.
levers.review: Controls operational cost leverage vs. financial risk and recruitment flexibility. Weakness: The options fail to consider how staffing continuity and technician expertise develop more robustly with larger active cohorts, or whether smaller operations in year 2 may hamper publication output and competitiveness for phase-two funding relative to larger competing residential facilities.

levers.name: Sensor Primacy Versus Human Technician Adjudication in Event Detection
levers.consequences: Immediate: Shifting reliance from technician visual/audio flagging toward mattress-sensor thresholds and automated alerts reduces nightly review cognitive load by an estimated 40–50% but increases false-positive escalation of ambiguous posture changes that mimic arousal. Systemic: Over a 3-year participant cohort, this trade-off between technician burnout prevention and annotation-rework cost accumulates; heavy sensor bias may require 10–15% more dual-scoring labor to validate borderline alerts, offsetting staffing relief. Strategic: A sensor-first posture locks the facility into expensive middleware tuning and vendor lock-in risk, while human-first primacy preserves methodological flexibility and publication credibility but risks technician fatigue and turnover.
options: Deploy mattress and infrared sensors as primary event triggers, with human technician review as secondary confirmation step, accepting 25–30% false-positive escalation to PSG in exchange for reduced nightly manual surveillance demands.

Maintain technician visual/audio observation as the gold standard for event flagging, with sensors serving as documentation and post-hoc pattern mining, accepting higher nightly staffing intensity but ensuring publication defensibility and lower annotation rework.

Institute a hybrid adaptive model where sensor confidence scores automatically gate technician review thresholds during high-activity nights (>5 potential events flagged), but revert to human-first gatekeeping during low-activity periods, requiring real-time threshold recalibration and periodic audits.
levers.review: Controls technician workload vs. annotation accuracy and publication defensibility. Weakness: The options fail to consider the clinical safety implications of missed real events during high-burden nights or the participant experience of excessive false-alarm PSG escalations, which may drive study dropout.

levers.name: Inclusion Criterion Selectivity and Enrollment Velocity Trade-Off
levers.consequences: Immediate: Strict pre-admission screening (requiring prior documented episodes, bed-partner collateral, and exclusion-checklist adjudication) ensures high phenotypic homogeneity and reduces protocol deviations, but delays recruitment by an estimated 3–5 weeks per participant. Systemic: Over the 3-year enrollment target of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants, a 4-week average recruitment delay compresses usable data-collection time by approximately 15–20% relative to looser screening, potentially reducing captured event yield by 12–18% if stay duration is capped at 10 weeks. Strategic: Strict gatekeeping protects Aim 1 operational validation and publication rigor but risks undershooting the 40+ events threshold that triggers external program review at month 18, forcing either stay-duration extension (cost increase) or recruitment-criteria loosening (methodological compromise).
options: Enforce full pre-admission screening including prior polysomnography report review, structured clinician-led interviews, and mandatory collateral history, accepting 4–6 week recruitment cycles in exchange for phenotypic confidence and minimal protocol amendments.

Streamline intake to a brief telephone interview and self-report questionnaire, with post-admission polysomnography on night 1 serving as the confirmatory gate for stay continuation, reducing recruitment delay to 1–2 weeks but risking 10–15% participant exclusion after enrollment and associated data-collection inefficiency.

Implement a two-tiered pathway: routine cases admitted after phone screening alone, with enhanced pre-screening reserved for borderline or high-comorbidity referrals, creating a population mix of high-confidence and exploratory cohorts analyzed separately to balance velocity against phenotypic clarity.
levers.review: Controls enrollment speed vs. phenotypic homogeneity and event-yield predictability. Weakness: The options fail to consider how recruitment momentum and early success stories (or failures) impact the credibility of referral pathways and institutional relationships with the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network.

levers.name: Matched Control Accrual and Baseline-Phenotyping Resource Allocation
levers.consequences: Immediate: Recruiting and hosting a matched control group (estimated 15–20 adults without parasomnia history) consumes approximately 10–15% of facility bed-nights and technician annotation capacity. Systemic: Control data enable rigorous sensor false-positive benchmarking and differential diagnosis learning (Aim 3 algorithm sensitivity/specificity), reducing semi-automated tool claims from 'promising indicators' to 'validated against normative baseline' — a shift worth 1–2 high-impact publications and approximately €200–300K in follow-on grant leverage. Strategic: Deferring controls to year 2 or outsourcing baseline collection to a separate institution saves immediate cash and accelerates case enrollment but surrenders first-publication priority and forces methodological dependence on external collaborators, weakening Bonn's hold on the algorithm IP narrative.
options: Recruit and host controls concurrently with case participants from month 1, distributing bed-night load and enabling real-time algorithm benchmarking at the cost of slower case accrual and compressed case stay duration.

Defer controls until year 2, after case phenotyping is established, allowing year-1 case enrollment to saturate available capacity and maximize within-person longitudinal depth, then conduct controls as a shorter burst study with dedicated summer staff resources.

Establish a remote control-monitoring arm where matched controls perform identical at-home monitoring using a subset of non-video sensors (EEG headband, mattress, microphone) without facility residence, reducing facility burden by 80% but limiting data quality and introducing recruitment attrition from non-residential inconvenience.
levers.review: Controls facility capacity utilization vs. algorithm-validation rigor and publication impact. Weakness: The options fail to consider how control data latency affects interim algorithm iterations and whether year-1 case phenotyping sufficiently matures to define meaningful control-matching criteria without seeing control data first.

levers.name: RBD Protocol Integration Versus Standalone Neurologic Screening Separation
levers.consequences: Immediate: Incorporating REM sleep behavior disorder under a secondary, compartmentalized protocol (with dedicated neurologic screening per AASM guidance and distinct inclusion/exclusion criteria) adds an estimated €150–200K year-1 overhead for additional clinician review, separate consent documentation, and confound-management procedures.
