Systemic: RBD participants require specialized safety precautions (fall risk, injury thresholds) and distinct sleep-stage montage prioritization; integrating RBD into the main night-technician workflow increases procedural overhead and context-switching by approximately 20–30% per shift, likely pushing technician FTE demand from 3.0 to 3.3–3.4. Strategic: Launching RBD as a parallel subprogram signals scientific ambition and broadens publication scope (neurodegenerative implications, RBD-to-PD conversion patterns), but delays year-1 focus on core NREM parasomnia validation; alternatively, NREM-only year 1 with RBD deferred to year 2 reduces risk and concentrates resource but sacrifices early interdisciplinary visibility.
options: Operate NREM and RBD as fully integrated protocols within the same 8-suite facility, with unified night-technician training covering both condition-specific safety requirements and diagnostic polysomnographic montages, accepting higher staff cognitive load but maximizing facility versatility and publication breadth.

Launch NREM parasomnia only in year 1, with RBD explicitly deferred to a year-2 competitive pilot phase contingent on successful NREM methods validation and demonstrated technician competency, preserving focus and reducing year-1 overhead by €150K.

Establish RBD as a separate administrative protocol overseen by a partnering neurologist at University Hospital Bonn, with RBD participants using the facility's equipment and infrastructure but following distinct enrollment, consent, and safety procedures managed outside the core Bonn unit team, creating operational separation at the cost of administrative duplication.
levers.review: Controls research scope and publication ambition vs. year-1 operational focus and technician cognitive workload. Weakness: The options fail to consider whether the facility's existing safety infrastructure and technician training adequately address RBD-specific fall and injury risk, or whether liability insurance and ethics-approval contingencies differ substantively between NREM and RBD cohorts.

levers.name: De-Identification and Data-Sharing Governance Pathway
levers.consequences: Immediate: Strict de-identification (removing video entirely, pseudonymizing physiological streams, restricting secondary use via data use agreements) ensures GDPR/ethics compliance and participant privacy protection but prevents post-hoc qualitative validation of sensor-flagged events against video ground truth and limits the richness of phenotypic analysis (e.g., behavioral context, injury mechanism reconstruction). Systemic: A permissive sharing policy (physiological + de-identified video metadata, with tiered access via formal DUA process) accelerates algorithm benchmarking and attracts secondary-analysis collaborators, potentially yielding 1–2 additional high-impact publications per year but increases participant privacy concerns and requires ongoing data governance oversight (estimated €40–60K year-1 compliance labor). Strategic: Conservative de-identification maximizes participant trust and reduces regulatory exposure but cedes competitive advantage in algorithm development to institutions with less-constrained datasets; permissive sharing positions Bonn as a central hub but invites mission creep and de-identification breach risk.
options: Deposit only EEG, movement, and environmental time-series data in public repositories under BIDS-NIFTI standards, with video confined to local secure storage accessible only to Bonn team members under strict ethics oversight, accepting limited external algorithm collaboration but preserving participant privacy maximally.

Implement a tiered-access model: physiological data and de-identified video metadata (e.g., frame-level motion scores, event timestamps) shared under formal DUA with academic institutions, while raw video remains on-premises for Bonn-internal validation only, balancing transparency and secondary-use opportunity against privacy.

Establish a federated-learning framework where external collaborators submit algorithm code to the Bonn data server for local execution and return only aggregate accuracy metrics and aggregate embeddings, rather than exporting raw data, eliminating traditional data export but requiring substantial middleware investment (€80–120K development).
levers.review: Controls participant privacy and regulatory compliance vs. secondary-use scientific leverage and algorithm external validation. Weakness: The options fail to consider heterogeneous participant willingness to share video (some may consent, others refuse), and whether ethical approval permits selective tiering based on individual consent preference or requires uniform de-identification across all participants.

levers.name: Algorithm Ownership and Pharma-Partnership IP Alignment
levers.consequences: Immediate: Securing an optional pharmaceutical partnership (estimated €400–500K additional funding) introduces contractual IP provisions; a default clause offering pharma a 30-day pre-publication comment window is lightweight, but pharma pressure for exclusive access to algorithm code or training data during the 3-year window constrains Bonn's publication timeline and secondary-collaboration options. Systemic: Pharma partnership accelerates algorithm validation work (dedicated computational resources, industry benchmark datasets) and could yield a high-impact algorithm validation paper by month 24, but any exclusive data/code restrictions delay open-source release by an estimated 6–12 months, reducing first-mover advantage in community adoption and follow-on method papers. Strategic: Accepting pharma funding without strict IP guardrails risks subordinating academic publication priorities to industry timelines; conversely, rejecting industry co-funding reduces budget flexibility (forcing more conservative year-2 scope or RBD deferral) but preserves unfettered publication freedom and positions Bonn as an independent data steward.
options: Pursue pharma funding with a contractual ceiling on exclusivity: 30-day pre-publication review window only, no exclusive data access, and immediate open-source algorithm release upon publication, accepting a modest funding amount (€250–300K) if full-scale pharma commitment is unavailable.

Decline pharma funding entirely, relying solely on DFG and University of Bonn resources, maintaining absolute publication autonomy and algorithm open-source status from month 12 onward, accepting tighter year-2 and year-3 budgets and possible scope reduction (e.g., RBD deferral).

Negotiate a full pharma partnership with a formal technology-development agreement: pharma receives 18-month exclusive algorithm access and co-author visibility on methods papers, in exchange for €500K funding and dedicated pharma computational resources, accepting delayed public release but securing substantial capital and industry-grade validation work.
levers.review: Controls academic independence and publication velocity vs. computational resources and algorithm validation rigor. Weakness: The options fail to consider the reputational and recruitment risk if pharma involvement becomes public and influences clinician referrals or participant enrollment, or whether DFG and University of Bonn stakeholders have institutional preferences about pharma partnerships.

levers.name: Residential Facility Expansion Trigger and Capacity-Scaling Decision Point
levers.consequences: Immediate: Deferring expansion from 8 to 12 suites until after month 18–24 validation (current plan) requires demonstrable success: ≥25 enrolled participants, ≥40 captured events, and manageable technician and annotation workload on the current 8-suite model. Systemic: A month-18 go/no-go expansion decision compounds with recruitment velocity and event-yield assumptions; if case accrual lags (e.g., only 18 participants by month 18), expanding to 12 suites commits an additional €300–400K capital (4 new suites, renovation, equipment) without demand certainty, risking stranded infrastructure and budget pressure. Strategic: Immediate expansion to 12 suites at launch (an additional €300K upfront) accelerates case enrollment and event capture, shortening the path to Aim 2 phenotyping conclusions and hypothesis-generating publications, but front-loads risk if recruitment proves slower or false-alarm rates force extensive facility repairs, and reduces DFG's flexibility to reallocate funds if early operational issues arise.
options: Maintain 8-suite operations through month 20, with a formal month-18 external-board review decision on 12-suite expansion contingent on meeting enrollment and event-capture targets, preserving budget optionality and risk mitigation at the cost of slower case throughput if demand is high.

Construct and equip 4 additional suites in parallel with year-1 operations (phased renovation starting month 3), maintaining year-1 staffing at 3.0 FTE but expanding to 4.0–4.2 FTE in year 2, accepting frontend capital commitment but ensuring capacity-ready infrastructure if recruitment accelerates.

Lease a second adjacent property in the Bonn neighborhood starting year 2, creating a geographically distributed 8 + 8 suite model with shared support services and cross-facility technician coverage, enabling 60%+ capacity scaling without on-site construction but introducing operational complexity and geographic dispersion of participant safety oversight.
levers.review: Controls upfront capital commitment and budget optionality vs. capacity readiness and enrollment throughput potential. Weakness: The options fail to consider how facility reputation and word-of-mouth recruitment momentum among referring clinicians may suddenly accelerate demand after the first 6 months, stranding the 8-suite model if expansion cannot be fast-tracked, or whether distributed multi-site operations degrade data quality and technician training coherence.

levers.name: Dual-Scorer Reliability Threshold and Adjudication Consensus Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Setting inter-rater agreement targets (e.g., Cohen's kappa ≥0.70 vs. ≥0.85) determines how frequently events are re-scored or arbitrated by a third clinician. Systemic: Tighter thresholds reduce disputed events by an estimated 15–25% but increase scoring labor by approximately 30–40 hours per month, consuming 10–15% of the annual independent-scoring budget (~€15K–€25K annually). Strategic: High consensus standards strengthen publication credibility and algorithmic training data quality, but lenient standards accelerate time-to-analysis and reduce the risk of recruitment stalling if event yield is lower than projected.
options: Adopt a two-tiered adjudication protocol in which events below the kappa threshold automatically escalate to PI review, with consensus required before inclusion in phenotyping analysis, prioritizing methodological rigor over speed.

Implement a pragmatic consensus rule in which disagreements between the two primary scorers are resolved by majority-rule category assignment (e.g., if one scorer flags a confusional arousal and the other flags a sleep terror, assign the more conservative label) without third-party arbitration, balancing cost and timeline.

Establish a machine-assisted disambiguation workflow in which disputed events are re-reviewed jointly by both scorers using side-by-side video and signal playback with an algorithm-generated confidence score displayed, aiming to achieve consensus without escalation in 80% of cases.
levers.review: Controls methodological rigor (stringent consensus) vs. operational feasibility (pragmatic dispute resolution). Weakness: The options fail to consider how partial arousal events or ambiguous polysomnographic morphology (e.g., slow-wave sleep with movement) will be classified, which may inflate disagreement rates independent of scorer training quality.

levers.name: Matched Control Recruitment Intensity and Baseline Phenotype Depth
levers.consequences: Immediate: Recruiting matched controls (age, sex, BMI, sleep-disorder-free status confirmed by screening questionnaire) from hospital employee pools, university staff, and community volunteers requires dedicated coordinator time for outreach and screening. Systemic: A 1:1 matched control-to-case ratio demands ~50–70 additional overnight stays (~10,000–14,000 sensor-monitoring nights) beyond the parasomnia cohort, increasing facility occupancy by 25–35%, but provides robust benchmark data for sensor false-positive rates and normative arousal baselines, reducing algorithmic training bias by an estimated 20–30%. Strategic: Comprehensive control recruitment strengthens the event-triage tool's specificity and the paper's methodological credibility, but delays phenotyping analysis by 6–12 months and strains night-technician capacity if controls are not staggered across years two and three.
options: Prioritize complete 1:1 matching throughout the 3-year study, with matched controls recruited in parallel to parasomnia participants and distributed evenly across years two and three, ensuring every case has a contemporaneous control baseline for cross-validation.

Implement a deferred matching strategy in which controls are recruited en masse in year two after parasomnia cohort enrollment stabilizes, allowing year-one operations to focus on case accrual and facility optimization before committing control-recruitment resources.

Adopt a minimal-matching approach targeting 1:2 case-to-control ratio using historical normative polysomnography data from the Bonn hospital's sleep clinic archive rather than new overnight stays, supplemented by only 15–20 new controls to validate sensor accuracy.
levers.review: Controls scientific rigor (contemporaneous 1:1 matched controls) vs. operational resource concentration (deferred or minimal matching). Weakness: The options fail to address whether controls should undergo identical enhanced-night PSG protocols and escalation-night procedures or whether a simpler single-night monitoring schema would suffice, which materially affects staffing load and comparability.

levers.name: Pharmaceutical Partnership Sequencing and Publication Autonomy Safeguards
levers.consequences: Immediate: Early-stage pharma partnership engagement (months 3–6) secures co-funding (~€400K–€600K annually) and drug-company interest in phenotyping results, but introduces pre-publication review periods and potential scope redirection toward drug-relevant sub-phenotypes. Systemic: A 30-day pre-publication comment window without veto rights preserves the team's autonomy but could delay major publications by 1–3 months; conversely, deferring partnership until year two allows methodological independence in early publications but risks losing pharma interest if preliminary findings are already public, reducing supplementary funding by an estimated 40–50%. Strategic: Early partnership de-risks the year-three budget contingency and accelerates infrastructure development, but late partnership maximizes publication independence and recruitment flexibility, though it increases reliance on DFG/university funding renewal.
options: Initiate partnership discussions immediately post-ethics approval (month 4–5) with a pharma company's sleep medicine division, negotiate a sponsored-research agreement with explicit carve-outs for publication timeline and scope, and deploy partnership funding to accelerate sensor validation and technician hiring in year one.

Delay partnership engagement until month 18 (mid-year two) once preliminary longitudinal data, event-capture rates, and early algorithmic results are available, allowing the academic team to publish foundational methods papers independently and then invite pharma interest from a position of demonstrated capability.

Pursue a lightweight industry-collaboration model in which equipment vendors (e.g., dry-electrode EEG headband manufacturers, mattress-sensor companies) provide in-kind hardware and co-authorship on methods papers in exchange for field validation data, avoiding direct pharma funding and its governance overhead.
levers.review: Controls publication autonomy (no pharma pre-publication review) vs. financial de-risking (pharma co-funding accelerates year-three contingency coverage). Weakness: The options fail to specify how recruitment messaging, inclusion-criterion scope, or long-term follow-up study design might shift if a pharma partner has strategic interest in specific parasomnia phenotypes or pharmaceutical endpoints.

levers.name: Sensor Hardware Procurement Model and Sensor Reliability Benchmarking
levers.consequences: Immediate: Procuring dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, and infrared cameras either as a single integrated platform (e.g., one vendor suite) or as best-of-class components from multiple suppliers with custom middleware. Systemic: Single-vendor integration reduces software debugging and inter-device calibration time by an estimated 25–30% and simplifies vendor support, but locks the facility into one manufacturer's product roadmap and pricing; multi-vendor modular procurement provides technology flexibility and lowers per-unit cost by 10–15% but demands 200–300 additional engineering hours (€15K–€25K) for middleware development and ongoing cross-vendor troubleshooting. Strategic: Single-vendor standardization accelerates the pilot-phase validation timeline and reduces startup risk, while multi-vendor modularity preserves long-term technology agility and prevents vendor lock-in, critical if a sensor platform is discontinued or major software flaws emerge.
options: Select a single integrated sensor platform (e.g., a vendor offering EEG headband, mattress pressure sensors, and video recording with unified cloud synchronization) with a 3-year commercial license, prioritizing turnkey reliability and vendor technical support over absolute cost minimization.

Assemble a modular sensor architecture from leading point-solutions in each category (premium dry-EEG headband, industrial-grade pressure-mat sensors, clinical-standard infrared camera, open-source time-synchronization middleware) with a data engineer dedicated to middleware maintenance, accepting higher upfront engineering cost for maximum long-term flexibility.

Establish a hardware co-development partnership with two sensor manufacturers in which the facility provides design feedback, field validation data, and early-adopter deployment in exchange for discounted hardware and embedded engineering support, positioning Bonn as a reference site for product maturation.
levers.review: Controls vendor lock-in risk (modular multi-vendor) vs. operational simplicity (single integrated platform). Weakness: The options fail to address how sensor failures, firmware updates, or unexpected incompatibilities will be diagnosed and resolved during nights when the facility is fully occupied, or how backup hardware will be stockpiled and maintained.

levers.name: Residential Environment Authenticity Versus Clinical Monitoring Standardization
levers.consequences: Immediate: Balancing 'domestic feel' (soft lighting, resident-style furnishings, unsupervised common areas, flexible bedtimes) against standardized clinical conditions (standardized room temperature 20–22°C, identical bedding, noise thresholds, coordinated lighting schedules) determines participant naturalness and sensor artifact. Systemic: Prioritizing authenticity increases homesickness-related early withdrawals and participant-reported sleep quality variability by an estimated 10–20%, but boosts ecological validity and captures sleep behavior closer to home conditions; conversely, strict standardization reduces withdrawal risk by 5–10% and improves signal consistency but may suppress parasomnia expression if the environment is perceived as too clinical, potentially reducing event yield by 15–25%. Strategic: High authenticity supports Aim 2 (longitudinal phenotyping) by capturing naturalistic within-person triggers and clustering patterns, while standardization strengthens Aim 3 (event-triage algorithm training) by reducing sensor noise and confounding environmental variation.
options: Design sleep suites with hotel-style comfort (blackout-capable but aesthetic window dressing, personal climate controls in the 18–24°C range, individualized bedding preferences with provided hypoallergenic options, soft ambient lighting with dimmer controls) and common areas mimicking a boutique residential setting, accepting 15–20% higher environmental variability in exchange for reduced study dropout.

Establish standardized clinical suite conditions with objective environmental monitoring (±1°C temperature tolerance, sound levels <40 dB during sleep, LED light spectrum matched to circadian research standards, hospital-grade bedding and linens) and mandatory participant briefing on sleep hygiene, prioritizing algorithm-training data quality over comfort-driven authenticity.

Implement a two-phase environment protocol in which participants undergo an initial 3-night acclimatization stay in a comfort-prioritized residential suite (weeks 1–2), then transition to a standardized clinical monitoring suite for weeks 3–8, allowing naturalistic baseline assessment followed by controlled phenotyping capture.
levers.review: Controls naturalistic event expression (comfort-prioritized environment) vs. sensor-data consistency (standardized clinical conditions). Weakness: The options fail to consider how individual circadian preference (chronotype), prior hospitalization anxiety, or unfamiliar bed-sharing arrangements with roommates will affect parasomnia expression independent of room environment, limiting the validity of environmental standardization.

levers.name: Night-Technician Scope Expansion and Real-Time Annotation Authority
levers.consequences: Immediate: Defining whether night technicians perform real-time event annotation using AASM criteria (flagging suspected episodes, recording timestamp and morphology live) or instead maintain passive observation logs deferred to daytime scorers. Systemic: Real-time AASM annotation by technicians requires 6–8 weeks of additional training (€3K–€5K cost and hiring delay) and increases nightly documentation time by 30–40 minutes per event, but accelerates escalation-night triggering and may reduce post-hoc scoring ambiguity by 10–20% because technician context is captured contemporaneously; passive logging reduces technician cognitive load and training burden but introduces a 12–24 hour lag before events are formally categorized, risking sleep-state context loss and escalation delays. Strategic: Real-time annotation authority empowers technicians as front-line clinicians and improves event-response speed, but may introduce systematic bias if technician training quality is heterogeneous, while passive observation maintains rigor but concentrates scoring burden on daytime staff and may miss subtle event recurrences requiring urgent escalation.
options: Invest in comprehensive AASM-criteria training for all night technicians (8-week program delivered by a board-certified sleep specialist, with competency testing and annual retraining) and authorize real-time event flagging with technician-recorded morphology descriptions, enabling same-night escalation decisions and contextual annotation.

Maintain night technicians as objective monitors who record video timestamps and physiological signal excerpts without categorical event assignment, with all AASM classification and escalation decisions deferred to the next-morning PI review, protecting scoring independence but accepting the 12–24 hour lag.

Establish a hybrid protocol in which technicians receive brief 2-week training on parasomnia-specific red flags (e.g., patient climbing out of bed, audible vocalization, thrashing leg movement) and are authorized to flag 'high-probability events' for immediate video review by an on-call clinician, stopping short of formal AASM diagnosis.
levers.review: Controls technician autonomy and event-response speed (real-time AASM annotation) vs. scoring rigor and consistency (deferred daytime classification). Weakness: The options fail to address how technician fatigue during the 8-hour shift will degrade event detection sensitivity, or how to maintain annotation quality if technicians work non-overlapping week-long rotations and lose continuity of individual participant behavior.

levers.name: Algorithm Development Governance and External Validation Pathway
levers.consequences: Immediate: Developing the semi-automated event-triage tool either in-house using dual-scorer ground truth (slower, lower risk of premature deployment, full academic control) or in partnership with a machine-learning-focused academic or industry lab (faster, higher external credibility risk if partner prioritizes generalization over parasomnia specificity). Systemic: In-house development by the postdoctoral computational neuroscientist takes approximately 12–15 months and delays algorithm benchmarking to month 24, but ensures full intellectual ownership and alignment with AASM criteria; external partnership can accelerate algorithm readiness to month 18–20 but introduces 15–20% risk of algorithm drift toward pharmaceutical or diagnostic-market objectives misaligned with the study's research aims, and requires formal data-sharing and IP agreements (€10K–€20K legal cost). Strategic: In-house development positions the team for follow-on grant funding and licensing partnerships, while external partnership leverages specialized ML expertise and accelerates publication, but may compromise the facility's independence and create conflicts with publication timelines if the partner has commercial interests.
options: Retain full in-house algorithm development led by the postdoctoral computational neuroscientist using dual-scored parasomnia events as ground truth, publish the algorithm and benchmarking results as an open-science repository with code and weights shared under a CC-BY license, and pursue licensing only after peer-reviewed validation is complete.

Establish a structured collaboration with a sleep-informatics research group at another European university (e.g., Leiden, Amsterdam, or Zürich) with complementary expertise in physiological signal processing, jointly own the algorithm intellectual property with pre-agreed publication timeline and co-authorship, and cross-validate on both Bonn and partner cohorts.

Engage a specialized medical-AI consultancy to develop the triage algorithm using federated learning, in which the algorithm learns from Bonn data without raw physiological signals leaving the facility, with the consultancy retaining algorithm architecture IP but the facility retaining full control of parasomnia-specific models and publication rights.
levers.review: Controls intellectual ownership and publication autonomy (in-house development) vs. algorithmic sophistication and timeline acceleration (external partnership). Weakness: The options fail to specify how the algorithm will be prospectively validated on new incoming participants in real-time, or whether external validation cohorts (e.g., other European sleep centers) will be solicited, which is critical for assessing generalization and supporting a future multi-center expansion grant.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences MUST:
     • Chain three SPECIFIC effects: "Immediate: [effect] → Systemic: [impact] → Strategic: [implication]"
     • Include measurable outcomes: "Systemic: [a specific, domain-relevant second-order impact with a measurable indicator, such as a % change, capacity shift, or cost delta]"
     • Explicitly describe trade-offs between core tensions
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional/innovative approach
     • Show clear progression: conservative → moderate → radical
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     • State the trade-off explicitly: "Controls [Tension A] vs. [Tension B]."
     • Identify a specific weakness: "Weakness: The options fail to consider [specific factor]."
   - For `summary`:
     • Identify ONE critical missing dimension
     • Prescribe CONCRETE addition: "Add '[full strategic option]' to [lever]"

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:", "Conservative:", "Moderate:", "Radical:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences
   - NO "[specific innovative option]" placeholders
   - NO value sets without clear strategic progression

6. **Option Structure Enforcement**
   - Radical option must include emerging tech/business model
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions

File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.
